cause: Recurrent transient unilateral arm numbness — suspected TIA vs benign paresthesias
HPI: The patient presents with two episodes this week of left arm numbness and tingling, each lasting approximately 15 minutes with complete resolution. They are currently asymptomatic. They deny any weakness, facial droop, speech difficulty, or vision changes during the episodes. The patient is a non-smoker with no history of diabetes, hypertension, cardiac disease, or hypercoagulability. They take no medications and deny oral contraceptive use or illicit substance use.
Objective: Vitals: HR 72, BP 128/82, RR 14, SpO2 99% on RA. The patient is alert and oriented x4, GCS 15. Neurological exam is entirely normal: cranial nerves II-XII intact, motor strength 5/5 in all extremities, sensation intact to light touch bilaterally, reflexes symmetric, gait steady. No facial asymmetry. No carotid bruits.
Diagnostics: ECG reveals Normal Sinus Rhythm without atrial fibrillation or ST changes. Serum glucose is 94 mg/dL. Total cholesterol is 198 mg/dL. CBC and BMP are within normal limits.

cause: First-ever headache described as worst headache — tension-type features vs first/worst red flag
HPI: The patient presents with an 8-hour bilateral band-like pressure headache with gradual onset over 1 hour. The patient states this is the first headache they have ever experienced and describes it as the worst headache they have ever had, though they note they do not typically get headaches. They deny fever, neck stiffness, vision changes, weakness, numbness, vomiting, photophobia, or recent trauma. No prior headache history. No medications.
Objective: Vitals: HR 74, BP 128/82, RR 14, Temp 98.4F, SpO2 99% on RA. The patient is alert, oriented x4, GCS 15. No meningismus. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Kernig and Brudzinski signs are negative.
Diagnostics: CBC shows WBC 7.8 x10^9/L (normal). BMP shows sodium 140 mEq/L, glucose 96 mg/dL — within normal limits.

cause: Right lower quadrant pain with leukocytosis — early appendicitis vs resolving gastroenteritis
HPI: The patient presents with right lower quadrant pain that began yesterday, described as a dull ache rated 5/10. They report nausea and decreased appetite but no vomiting. They had a stomach bug approximately one week ago that has mostly resolved. They deny dysuria, hematuria, or changes in bowel habits. No prior abdominal surgeries. Past medical history is unremarkable.
Objective: Vitals: HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 16, Temp 99.8F (37.7C), SpO2 99% on RA. Abdomen is soft and non-distended. There is tenderness to palpation in the right lower quadrant. Murphy sign is negative. No rebound tenderness. No guarding. Bowel sounds are present.
Diagnostics: CBC shows WBC 12.4 x10^9/L (elevated). Urinalysis is normal. BMP is within normal limits. Lipase is within normal limits. CT abdomen/pelvis is pending.

cause: Unilateral calf swelling post-travel with elevated D-dimer — DVT requiring anticoagulation initiation
HPI: The patient presents with 2 days of progressive left calf swelling and soreness that began following a 6-hour flight. They note the left calf appears larger than the right. They deny chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or pleuritic pain. They have no personal or family history of clotting disorders, no prior DVT or PE, and are not on anticoagulation. They take no regular medications.
Objective: Vitals: HR 88, BP 124/76, RR 14, SpO2 98%, Temp 98.7F on RA. The left calf is visibly swollen, tender to palpation, and measures approximately 2 cm larger in circumference than the right. No erythema. The foot is warm with normal distal pulses. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No tachypnea.
Diagnostics: D-dimer is elevated at 680 ng/mL. CBC is within normal limits. Lower extremity venous duplex ultrasound is pending.

cause: Hyperglycemia with ketosis in Type 1 diabetes — evolving DKA
HPI: The patient presents with polydipsia, polyuria, nausea, and mild abdominal discomfort since this morning. They have a known history of Type 1 diabetes mellitus. They are not confused and report tolerating sips of water. They deny fever, vomiting, chest pain, or shortness of breath. They report recent difficulty managing blood sugars over the past 3 days and admit to missing two insulin doses.
Objective: Vitals: HR 104, BP 118/74, RR 20, Temp 98.7F, SpO2 98% on RA. The patient appears mildly dehydrated with dry mucous membranes. Abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound or guarding. Lungs are clear. The patient is alert, oriented x4, and conversationally appropriate.
Diagnostics: Serum glucose is 320 mg/dL. Venous pH is 7.31. Beta-hydroxybutyrate is 2.8 mmol/L. Anion gap is 18. Serum bicarbonate is 18 mmol/L. Potassium is 4.8 mEq/L. Creatinine is 1.2 mg/dL. CBC is within normal limits.

cause: Flank pain with fever and pyuria — acute pyelonephritis, oral vs IV antibiotics
HPI: The patient presents with 2 days of progressive dysuria followed by the onset of left flank pain and fever today. They report nausea but no vomiting and are currently tolerating oral fluids. They have no history of kidney stones, urinary tract anomalies, or recurrent UTIs. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. They take no regular medications.
Objective: Vitals: HR 96, BP 112/70, RR 16, Temp 100.4F (38.0C), SpO2 99% on RA. The patient appears uncomfortable but is in no acute distress. There is left costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion. Abdomen is soft, non-tender, without guarding or rebound. Lungs are clear.
Diagnostics: Urinalysis shows nitrites positive, leukocyte esterase positive, WBC 15-20/hpf. CBC shows WBC 11.8 x10^9/L (elevated). Creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (normal). Blood cultures are pending. Lactate is 1.2 mmol/L (normal).

cause: Mild thrombocytopenia on routine labs — likely post-viral transient suppression
HPI: The patient presents after routine bloodwork revealed a low platelet count. They report feeling entirely well. They had a bad cold approximately 2 weeks ago that has since fully resolved. They deny any nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in urine or stool, unusual bruising, petechiae, or prolonged bleeding from cuts. They are on no blood thinners or new medications. Past medical history is unremarkable.
Objective: Vitals: HR 74, BP 112/70, RR 14, SpO2 99% on RA. Physical exam reveals no petechiae, ecchymoses, or other signs of bleeding. No hepatosplenomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. The patient appears well.
Diagnostics: CBC shows platelets 128 x10^3/uL (low; reference 150-400), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL (normal), WBC 6.8 x10^3/uL (normal). INR is 1.0 (normal). BMP is within normal limits.

cause: Acute unilateral facial weakness — classic Bell's palsy with LMN pattern, needs steroids within 72h
HPI: The patient presents after waking up this morning with right-sided facial weakness. They are unable to fully close the right eye and note the mouth droops on the right side. They report that both the forehead and lower face are affected: weak eyebrow raise, incomplete eye closure, and mouth droop. They deny any arm or leg weakness, speech or comprehension difficulties, headache, vision changes, or dysphagia. They had a mild cold approximately 1 week ago. No history of diabetes, stroke, or cardiac disease.
Objective: Vitals: HR 72, BP 128/78, RR 14, Temp 98.6F (37.0C), SpO2 99% on RA. The patient is alert, oriented x4, GCS 15. Exam reveals right-sided facial weakness involving both the forehead and lower face consistent with a lower motor neuron pattern. Incomplete right eye closure is noted. No other cranial nerve deficits. Motor strength is 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation is intact. Gait is steady.
Diagnostics: Serum glucose is 94 mg/dL. HbA1c is 5.4%. CBC shows WBC 7.2 x10^9/L (normal). ESR is 12 mm/hr (normal).

source: Ramaswamy et al. 2026, Case 26 (Acute red eye)
cause: Painful red eye with decreased visual acuity — anterior uveitis vs corneal ulcer vs acute glaucoma
Chief Complaint: Painful red eye with blurry vision
HPI: The patient presents with 2 days of left eye redness, a deep ache described as not like something is in the eye, photophobia, and slightly blurry vision. They report the pain is constant and worsening. They deny discharge, crusting, or trauma. They have no history of autoimmune disease, do not wear contact lenses, and have no prior ophthalmologic conditions. Past medical history is unremarkable.
Objective: Vitals: HR 74, BP 122/76, RR 14, SpO2 99% on RA. Left eye exam reveals limbal injection (circumcorneal flush) with a miotic (constricted) pupil. No purulent or watery discharge. Visual acuity is 20/30 in the left eye (baseline 20/20 per patient report). Right eye exam is unremarkable with visual acuity 20/20.
Diagnostics: CBC shows WBC 6.8 x10^9/L (normal). ESR is 18 mm/hr (normal). CRP is 3.2 mg/L (normal). ANA is negative.

source: Ramaswamy et al. 2026, Case 27 (Septic arthritis vs gout)
cause: Hot swollen monoarthritis with low-grade fever — must rule out septic arthritis, needs arthrocentesis
HPI: The patient presents with 2 days of progressive right knee swelling, pain, and warmth. They can barely bend the knee. They have had a low-grade fever at home. They deny any remembered injury or trauma. They play recreational basketball regularly. They report social alcohol use. They have no history of gout, rheumatoid arthritis, or other joint conditions. No prior joint infections or surgeries. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable.
Objective: Vitals: HR 84, BP 126/78, RR 14, Temp 99.5F (37.5C), SpO2 99% on RA. Right knee demonstrates moderate effusion, warmth, and tenderness to palpation. Range of motion is significantly limited by pain. No tracking erythema or cellulitis of the surrounding skin. No other joints are affected. The patient is weight-bearing with an antalgic gait.
Diagnostics: CBC shows WBC 11.2 x10^9/L (borderline elevated). Uric acid is 7.8 mg/dL (elevated). ESR is 38 mm/hr (elevated). CRP is 42 mg/L (elevated). Blood cultures are pending. Joint aspiration is pending.

cause: Active suicidal ideation with identified method but no intent — self-initiated means restriction, functioning
HPI: The patient presents with 1 week of worsening depression and active suicidal ideation. They report having thought repeatedly about taking an overdose of pills but deny intent to act, stating the thoughts scare them. They have voluntarily given their medications to a family member as a self-initiated safety measure. They deny access to firearms. They have no prior suicide attempts. They deny substance use. They report still going to work and caring for dependents. Past psychiatric history includes depression managed with outpatient therapy; no prior hospitalizations.
Objective: Vitals: HR 72, BP 126/78, RR 14, Temp 98.6F, SpO2 99% on RA. The patient is tearful but cooperative. Cognitively intact with intact orientation, attention, and memory. No psychomotor agitation or retardation. No evidence of psychosis, paranoia, or hallucinations. Speech is fluent and goal-directed.
Diagnostics: TSH is 2.1 mIU/L (normal). Vitamin B12 is within normal limits. CBC and BMP are within normal limits. Urine drug screen is negative.

cause: First-episode psychosis — paranoid ideation, disorganized thoughts, auditory disturbance, family-supported
HPI: The patient is brought in by family members due to 2 months of progressive paranoid ideation. The patient believes people are watching and talking about them. They describe difficulty organizing their thoughts and report hearing their name called when no one is present, though they deny command hallucinations. They report declining sleep and appetite over this period. They deny suicidal or homicidal ideation. They are not intoxicated and deny drug use. They have no known medical or psychiatric history. Family members are present and supportive.
Objective: Vitals: HR 84, BP 122/70, RR 14, Temp 98.6F, SpO2 99% on RA. The patient is alert and oriented x3 (person, place, time). Speech is tangential but comprehensible. Affect is paranoid and guarded but the patient is cooperative with the interview. No psychomotor agitation. No focal neurologic deficits.
Diagnostics: Urine toxicology screen is negative. TSH is 1.7 mIU/L (normal). CBC, BMP, calcium, and glucose are all within normal limits.
